title_en: Animals Are the Original Wellness Influencers
title_zh: 动物是最早的健康潮流带头者

paragraphs.en: In the early 2010s, researchers in Mexico City noticed that sparrows and finches at the national university were lacing their nests with cigarette butts. The birds would collect the butts—mostly smoked—carefully remove the outer paper layer, and weave fibers from the filters into their homes, among the twigs and grass.
paragraphs.zh: 在2010年代初，墨西哥城的研究人员发现，国家大学里的麻雀和文雀会在筑巢时把烟蒂缝进鸟巢。鸟儿会捡起烟蒂——多数是抽过的——小心去掉外面的纸层，再把滤棒里的纤维织进它们的窝里，和树枝与草一起形成巢穴结构。

paragraphs.en: The line between science and wellness has been blurred beyond recognition. WIRED is here to help.
paragraphs.zh: 科学与健康观念之间的界限已被模糊得几乎无法辨认，Wired 来帮你厘清。

paragraphs.en: This sort of dubious yet intriguing lifestyle choice will be familiar to anyone who follows health trends. It seems weird—but does it make some kind of backward sense? In this case, the birds were vindicated: The more cigarette filter fibers the nests had, the fewer parasites they harbored, probably because nicotine repels bugs. There are drawbacks, though: Chicks raised in butt nests are more likely to develop blood cell abnormalities. Again, familiar.
paragraphs.zh: 这种看似可疑却颇有趣味的生活方式选择，对任何追随健康潮流的人来说都不陌生。它听起来怪怪的，但有没有某种“反向常识”的道理呢？在这个案例里，鸟类行为得到了证实：鸟巢里烟草滤纸纤维越多，它们携带的寄生虫就越少，这可能是因为尼古丁具有驱虫作用。不过也有副作用：在烟蒂巢里长大的雏鸟更容易出现血细胞异常。这点也很熟悉。

paragraphs.en: While we may not want to follow this particular lead, animals are the original wellness influencers. “Healers and shamans have looked at animals for thousands of years,” says biologist Jaap de Roode, author of the recent book Doctors by Nature. Some of these discoveries have trickled up: Oshá root—which, as indigenous Americans have long observed, bears like to chew up and rub on their fur—is available in many natural medicine stores for various uses, including pain relief. Other animal wellness trends might not be quite as imitable, sadly, for our species.
paragraphs.zh: 虽然我们未必想效仿这种做法，但动物确实是最早的健康博主。生物学家雅普·德·鲁德（Jaap de Roode）在其新作《Doctors by Nature》中说：“数千年来，治疗师和萨满都在观察动物。” 其中一些发现后来被人类借鉴：例如Oshá根——美洲原住民长期观察到浣熊会喜欢咬着它并用来摩擦毛发——现在在许多天然药店都能买到，被用于多种用途，包括止痛。遗憾的是，其他一些动物健康潮流对人类来说可能并不那么容易模仿。

paragraphs.en: Parasites are a top concern for animals and have inspired waves of evolutionary creativity. Some parasite-infected sea slugs shed their entire bodies, then regenerate from the head. But more common is what de Roode calls “animal medication.” Animals are considered to medicate when they eat or apply an external substance that they normally wouldn’t and it helps them “by preventing or clearing infection or reducing disease symptoms,” he says.
paragraphs.zh: 寄生虫始终是动物面临的一大威胁，也激发了持续不断的进化“创意”。有些被寄生虫感染的海蛞蝓会把整个身体脱落，再从头部再生。不过更常见的是德·鲁德所说的“动物用药行为”。当动物吃下或使用一种它们通常不会使用的外源物质，并且这样做能“预防或清除感染，或减轻疾病症状”时，科学家就认为它们在进行自我用药。

paragraphs.en: Over the past few decades, more studies have focused on animal medication in a particular group: insects. When woolly bear caterpillars are infected with fly maggots, they begin eating more alkaloid-heavy, parasite-killing plants with no nutritional value. Research has shown that infection changes the caterpillars’ buds so that the bitter plants “taste really good,” de Roode says, perhaps like a saltine when you’re finally kicking norovirus. Wood ants fill their nests with foraged spruce resin, which has antibacterial and antifungal effects.
paragraphs.zh: 在过去几十年里，越来越多的研究把注意力集中在一个特定群体的“动物用药”：昆虫。当羊毛熊幼虫感染蝇蛆时，它们会开始进食更多富含生物碱、但几乎没有营养价值、专门用于杀灭寄生虫的植物。研究表明，感染会改变这些幼虫的“芽芽/萌芽”状态，使得它们觉得苦味植物“味道很好吃”，德·鲁德说，这就像你在诺如病毒快要痊愈时终于能吃到盐味饼干一样。木蚁会把采集来的云杉树脂堆进巢穴中，而这种树脂具有抗菌和抗真菌的作用。

paragraphs.en: We can learn a lot from bug herbalists, de Roode says. The chemical mixes found in resins and plants may help other animals avoid the drug resistance humans run into with single-chemical medicines. And many insects invest in community and intergenerational health, practicing what some researchers call “social medication.” For instance, parasite-infected monarch butterfly moms lay their eggs on more medicinally powerful milkweed species, so their offspring won’t have to suffer like they do.
paragraphs.zh: 德·鲁德说，我们可以从这些“昆虫草药师”身上学到很多东西。树脂和植物里找到的化学混合物可能帮助其他动物避开人类单一化学药物所遭遇的耐药问题。许多昆虫还会在群体和代际健康上投入更多精力，实践着一些研究者称之为“社会用药”的行为。比如，受寄生虫感染的帝王蝶妈妈会把卵产在更具药效的马兜铃草种类上，以减少后代遭受和它们相同困境。

paragraphs.en: Closeness can help in more direct ways. Social animals, especially primates, also share wellness tricks with one another. Capuchin monkeys will rub themselves with extruded millipede toxin, which serves as a bug repellent and also gets them mildly high. Clusters of capuchins will pass around a potent ’pede.
paragraphs.zh: 亲密接触也能以更直接的方式发挥作用。社会性动物，尤其是灵长类，也会把各自的健康技巧彼此分享。悬臂猴会用挤压后的马陆毒素给自己擦拭，这种毒素既能驱赶昆虫，也能让它们产生轻微兴奋。成群的悬臂猴会互相递送一股效力很强的马陆制剂。

paragraphs.en: Great apes get wisdom from others through a behavior called “peering,” says primatologist Isabelle Laumer. When one ape is doing something, another will come close and watch them intently. Peering and other ways of teaching and learning have led primate groups to develop specific wellness cultures. Chimpanzees, gorillas, and bonobos deal with parasitic infections by gulping down hairy leaves, a practice so widespread it’s known as “leaf-swallowing.” As the leaves pass through the digestive tract, the leaf fuzz grabs parasitic worms and ferries them out. Different ape societies have different leaf-swallowing preferences, their equivalent of family chicken soup recipes.
paragraphs.zh: 灵长类动物行为学家伊莎贝尔·劳默称，大猩猩、黑猩猩和倭黑猩猩通过一种叫“偷看”(peering)的行为从彼此那里获得智慧。当一只灵长类正在做某件事，另一只会走近并认真观察它。这样的观察以及其他教学和学习方式，使灵长类群体逐渐形成了各自的健康文化。黑猩猩、猩猩和倭黑猩猩会通过吞食带毛叶子来对抗寄生虫感染，这一做法非常普遍，以至于被称为“吞叶疗法”。这些毛茸茸的叶片在消化道里会把寄生虫线虫缠住并带出去。不同的灵长类社会有不同的吞叶偏好，像是他们各自“家传鸡汤配方”的版本。

paragraphs.en: Also, innovation is a constant, for both human and animal wellness. Research that Laumer and others published in 2024 describes how an orangutan named Rakus made a poultice out of a chewed-up plant and applied it to a big gash on his face. The plant is known to be “anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, and pain-relieving,” Laumer says. Rakus turned it into a bandage—a behavior never seen before, meaning he may be an innovator in the orangutan wound care space. Will it become the new leaf-swallowing? Virality is notoriously hard to predict, but who knows—it has a shot.
paragraphs.zh: 同样，人类和动物的健康实践中，创新也一直在发生。劳默和其他人于2024年发表的研究描述了一只名叫Rakus的红毛猩猩：他把嚼碎的植物制成膏状药贴，并敷在自己脸上的一道大口子上。劳默说，这种植物已知具有“抗炎、抗菌、抗病毒、抗真菌和止痛”功效。Rakus把它变成了一种绷带，这种行为以前从未见过，这意味着他可能是红毛猩猩伤口护理领域的一位“创新者”。它会不会成为像“吞叶疗法”那样的新趋势？病毒式传播向来难以预测，但谁也说不准——它是有机会的。

paragraphs.en: Of course, wellness shouldn’t just be in response to sickness: It can be proactive, and even fun. Here, we can take inspiration from whales and their inventive exfoliation routines.
paragraphs.zh: 当然，健康并不应只在生病时才出现；它也可以是主动的，甚至是有趣的。在这里，我们可以从鲸鱼身上汲取灵感，学习它们发明性的去角质护肤习惯。

paragraphs.en: Ocean water is thick with viruses and bacteria, and whales must “shed continuously to maintain healthy skin” and get rid of barnacles, says marine ecologist Olaf Meynecke.
paragraphs.zh: 海洋水体中充满了病毒和细菌，海洋生态学家奥拉夫·迈内克称，鲸鱼必须“持续脱落皮肤表层”以维持皮肤健康，并清除藤壳虫类附着生物。

paragraphs.en: This is challenging for animals with such short limbs, so they have gotten creative. Bowheads in the Arctic rub themselves against craggy rocks, and orcas in the Antarctic do the same with icebergs. Another orca population, the northern residents of British Columbia, have perfected a technique called beach rubbing, where they gather to drag their bellies across smooth pebbles. Summer 2026 TikTok trend, anyone?
paragraphs.zh: 对四肢很短的动物来说这并不容易，所以它们不得不另辟蹊径。北极的抹香鲸会在崎岖的岩石上摩擦身体，南极的虎鲸也会对冰山做同样的动作。另一个虎鲸群体——不列颠哥伦比亚省北部的“本地居民”——已经把一种叫做“沙滩摩擦”的方法玩得炉火纯青：它们会聚在一起，把腹部拖过光滑的鹅卵石。2026年夏天TikTok流行趋势吗？

paragraphs.en: In Australia and elsewhere, groups of humpbacks drop down to sandy areas of the seabed and roll, sending “sand and skin flying around everywhere,” says Meynecke, who was the first to film them performing this behavior. (Fish flocked to eat the nutritious skin flakes.) “Maybe,” he muses, “it was a spa.” Humpbacks even supplement their sand treatments with “kelping”—playing around and rubbing themselves with the seaweed, which has antibacterial properties.
paragraphs.zh: 在澳大利亚以及其他地方，群体抹香鲸会下潜到海底沙质区域并打滚，梅内凯称此时会“到处飞溅起沙子和皮屑”。（鱼群会聚拢过来，来吃掉营养丰富的皮屑。）他沉思道：“也许，这就是一次水疗。”抹香鲸甚至用“海带疗法”来为沙疗做补充——它们会与海藻嬉戏并用身体摩擦海藻，而海藻本身具有抗菌特性。

paragraphs.en: Whales may go to the spa with their friends. For other animals, the spa is their friends. Social grooming—when community members lick each other, comb each other’s fur with their fingers, etc.—is a top activity for critters ranging from field mice (who flirt through grooming) and cows (who prefer to groom their twins) to female vampire bats (who will try to nibble off tracking devices that researchers place on their friends).
paragraphs.zh: 鲸鱼可能会和朋友一起去“美容护理”。对其他动物来说，朋友本身就是“美容院”。社会性梳理——即群体成员互相舔舐、用指头理毛等——是许多动物的重要活动，从田鼠（通过梳理来调情）和奶牛（更愿意给双胞胎同伴梳理）到雌性吸血蝙蝠（它们会试图咬掉研究人员给同伴套上的追踪器）都如此。

paragraphs.en: Grooming helps animals stay clean, but there are additional benefits. It can reduce stress for both participants by slaking a thirst for interaction, says animal behaviorist Gerald Carter. Having someone’s teeth and claws so near also “requires a level of tolerance and trust,” he adds, laying the groundwork for higher-stakes cooperation.
paragraphs.zh: 梳理不仅能帮助动物保持清洁，而且还有额外好处。动物行为学家杰拉德·卡特认为，它还能通过满足互动欲望来缓解双方的压力。卡特补充说，让对方的牙齿和爪子离自己这么近，也“需要一定的忍耐和信任”，这为更高风险的合作关系奠定了基础。

paragraphs.en: This is especially apparent with female vampire bats, where grooming relationships can graduate into food-sharing ones. If a bat doesn’t hunt successfully, her comrades will regurgitate blood into her mouth, potentially saving her life. In one of Carter’s experiments, vampire bats consistently spent about 4 percent of their waking time grooming each other whether they really needed it or not, strengthening those bonds for when it matters.
paragraphs.zh: 这种现象在雌性吸血蝙蝠身上尤其明显，因为彼此梳理毛毛关系有时会进一步发展为共食关系。如果一只蝙蝠没有成功猎食，她的同伴会把血液反刍到她嘴里，从而有可能拯救她的生命。在 Carter 的一项实验中，吸血蝙蝠不管是否真正需要，都几乎都把醒着时的大约4%时间花在彼此梳理对方毛发上，从而在关键时刻加固这种联系。

paragraphs.en: People can learn from this. Most measures of human health are “correlated with the quality and the quantity of your social relationships,” Carter says. While we may not demonstrate closeness by regurgitating blood, or even low-key mutual scratching, strong friendships mean better health.
paragraphs.zh: 人类也可以从中学习。卡特表示，人体健康的大多数指标都“与人际关系的质量和数量相关”。虽然我们可能不会像反刍血液那样表达亲密，甚至也不会进行“低调的互相抓挠”，但牢固的友谊意味着更好的健康。
